section.title: Crossing the Red Sea
section.sectionRationale: This is the decisive act of deliverance that gives Israel its identity as a redeemed people: trapped between Pharaoh's army and the sea, they watch the LORD fight for them and walk through the waters on dry ground. The New Testament treats this event as the paradigm for understanding salvation itself.
section.thesis: Cornered between the sea and Pharaoh's army, Israel is told to stand still and watch the LORD fight for them; He divides the sea, brings them through on dry ground, and destroys the pursuing army, so that Israel finally fears the LORD and believes in Him and in Moses His servant.
section.frameNote: Five movements: God's guidance by the longer road and the pillar of cloud and fire; Pharaoh's pursuit; Israel's terror and Moses' word of faith; the sea divided and crossed; and the sea returning to destroy the Egyptians while Israel believes.

units.label: God leads by the longer road
units.note: the pillar of cloud and fire

media.alt: A pillar of cloud by day and fire by night leading the people
media.caption: The LORD Himself goes before them, by day and by night.

verses.text: When Pharaoh let the people go, God did not lead them along the road through the land of the Philistines, though it was shorter. For God said, “If the people face war, they might change their minds and return to Egypt.”
So God led the people around by the way of the wilderness toward the Red Sea. And the Israelites left the land of Egypt arrayed for battle.

Moses took the bones of Joseph with him because Joseph had made the sons of Israel swear a solemn oath when he said, “God will surely attend to you, and then you must carry my bones with you from this place.”
They set out from Succoth and camped at Etham on the edge of the wilderness.
And the LORD went before them in a pillar of cloud to guide their way by day, and in a pillar of fire to give them light by night, so that they could travel by day or night.
Neither the pillar of cloud by day nor the pillar of fire by night left its place before the people.
Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Tell the Israelites to turn back and encamp before Pi-hahiroth, between Migdol and the sea. You are to encamp by the sea, directly opposite Baal-zephon.
For Pharaoh will say of the Israelites, ‘They are wandering the land in confusion; the wilderness has boxed them in.’

And I will harden Pharaoh's heart so that he will pursue them. But I will gain honor by means of Pharaoh and all his army, and the Egyptians will know that I am the LORD.” 
So this is what the Israelites did.
When the king of Egypt was told that the people had fled, Pharaoh and his officials changed their minds about them and said, “What have we done? We have released Israel from serving us.”
So Pharaoh prepared his chariot and took his army with him.
He took 600 of the best chariots, and all the other chariots of Egypt, with officers over all of them.
And the LORD hardened the heart of Pharaoh king of Egypt so that he pursued the Israelites, who were marching out defiantly.
The Egyptians, all Pharaoh's horses and chariots, horsemen and troops, pursued the Israelites and overtook them as they camped by the sea near Pi-hahiroth, opposite Baal-zephon.

units.label: Israel's fear, Moses' word of faith
units.note: stand still and see the salvation of the LORD

verses.text: As Pharaoh approached, the Israelites looked up and saw the Egyptians marching after them, and they were terrified and cried out to the LORD.
They said to Moses, “Was it because there were no graves in Egypt that you brought us into the wilderness to die? What have you done to us by bringing us out of Egypt?
Did we not say to you in Egypt, ‘Leave us alone so that we may serve the Egyptians’? For it would have been better for us to serve the Egyptians than to die in the wilderness.”

But Moses told the people, “Do not be afraid. Stand firm and you will see the LORD's {{salvation|salvation}}, which He will accomplish for you today; for the Egyptians you see today, you will never see again.
The LORD will fight for you; you need only to be still.”
Then the LORD said to Moses, “Why are you crying out to Me? Tell the Israelites to go forward.
And as for you, lift up your staff and stretch out your hand over the sea and divide it, so that the Israelites can go through the sea on dry ground.
And I will harden the hearts of the Egyptians so that they will go in after them. Then I will gain honor by means of Pharaoh and all his army and chariots and horsemen.
The Egyptians will know that I am the LORD when I am honored through Pharaoh, his chariots, and his horsemen.”
And the angel of God, who had gone before the camp of Israel, withdrew and went behind them. The pillar of cloud also moved from before them and stood behind them,
so that it came between the camps of Egypt and Israel. The cloud was there in the darkness, but it lit up the night. So all night long neither camp went near the other.
Then Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and all that night the LORD drove back the sea with a strong east wind that turned it into dry land. So the waters were divided,
and the Israelites went through the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on their right and on their left.
And the Egyptians chased after them, all Pharaoh's horses, chariots, and horsemen, and followed them into the sea.
At morning watch, however, the LORD looked down on the army of the Egyptians from the pillar of fire and cloud, and He threw their camp into confusion.
He caused their chariot wheels to wobble, so that they had difficulty driving. “Let us flee from the Israelites,” said the Egyptians, “for the LORD is fighting for them against Egypt!”
Then the LORD said to Moses, “Stretch out your hand over the sea, so that the waters may flow back over the Egyptians and their chariots and horsemen.”
So Moses stretched out his hand over the sea, and at daybreak the sea returned to its normal state. As the Egyptians were retreating, the LORD swept them into the sea.
The waters flowed back and covered the chariots and horsemen, the entire army of Pharaoh that had chased the Israelites into the sea. Not one of them survived.
But the Israelites had walked through the sea on dry ground, with walls of water on their right and on their left.
That day the LORD saved Israel from the hand of the Egyptians, and Israel saw the Egyptians dead on the shore.
When Israel saw the great power that the LORD had exercised over the Egyptians, the people {{feared|feared}} the LORD and {{believed|believed}} in Him and in His servant Moses.

salvation.gloss: Salvation, deliverance, rescue. The word shares its root with the name Joshua and, in Greek translation, lies behind the name Jesus, the LORD saves. Moses tells the terrified people to stand and watch this yeshuah unfold, a salvation they cannot achieve, only receive.

feared.gloss: To fear, to stand in reverent awe. After watching the LORD destroy Pharaoh's army, Israel's fear moves from terror of Egypt to reverence for God, the beginning of true worship.

believed.gloss: To believe, to trust, to be firm or faithful, root of the word amen. Israel's fear and faith are named together as the fruit of watching God act; belief here is not blind but grounded in what they had just seen Him do.

basic.context: Israel is trapped, the sea ahead, Pharaoh's army closing in behind. They panic and blame Moses, but Moses tells them to stand still and watch God save them. The LORD splits the sea, Israel walks through on dry ground, and the returning waters destroy the pursuing army completely.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a real, historical deliverance of a real, historical people at a real body of water. The New Testament treats it as the Old Testament's clearest picture of salvation: helpless people, an impossible barrier, and a God who fights and delivers when His people can do nothing but trust and obey.
basic.originalLanguages: Moses promises Israel they will see the LORD's yeshuah, salvation, using the very word later behind the names Joshua and Jesus. Watching God's power destroy Egypt, Israel finally yare, fears, the LORD rightly, and they aman, believe, trust Him, the same root behind the word amen.
basic.typology: Paul later calls this crossing a baptism into Moses, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2, an image the New Testament reapplies to baptism into Christ: passing through death-dealing waters into new life on the other side, while judgment falls on those left behind.
basic.christ: Just as Israel could only stand still and watch the LORD fight for them, salvation in Christ comes not by human effort but by trusting a Mediator who fights the real battle and wins it alone. The New Testament reads this whole episode as inviting a Christ-centered lens: get out of bondage, cross over by grace, all through a mediator's action, not your own.
basic.god: God is a warrior who fights for His helpless people, and He is also a God whose glory is displayed precisely in defeating those who oppose Him. His patience with Israel's fear and grumbling is matched by His relentless commitment to bring them all the way through.
basic.crossReferences: 1 Corinthians 10:1-2 calls this a baptism into Moses in the cloud and in the sea. Isaiah 51:9-11 recalls this crossing as the pattern for future redemption. Psalm 106:7-12 retells the story to teach later generations to trust God's saving power.
basic.oneStory: Cornered with no way out, Israel does nothing but stand still while God alone fights and saves. This becomes the Bible's template for salvation itself: helpless sinners, an impossible barrier, and a God who acts decisively through a mediator, ultimately fulfilled when Christ alone secures the deliverance His people could never achieve themselves.

context.literary: The narrative builds tension deliberately, God's own strategic misdirection of the route, Pharaoh's fresh pursuit, Israel's terrified accusation of Moses, before resolving in the extended, almost liturgical description of the sea dividing and returning.
context.historical: This event becomes Israel's defining national memory, recited in psalms and prophetic oracles for centuries afterward as proof of God's power to save and as the pattern for hoped-for future deliverances.
context.canonical: The New Testament treats the exodus, and this crossing especially, as the primary Old Testament lens for understanding salvation: bondage, a mediator, a crossing through judgment, and a people brought safely to the other side.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that Israel's deliverance came entirely from the LORD fighting on their behalf, not from their own strength or cleverness, and that this display of power was meant to produce lasting fear of God and faith in His word.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This passage narrates a specific, unrepeatable act of God in history; it is not a template guaranteeing believers will always be miraculously rescued from every danger. The enduring principle is not the miracle's mechanics but its message: God saves those who are helpless, and He alone deserves the fear and faith that follow.

debates.question: How should the hardening of Pharaoh's heart in verses 4, 8, and 17 be understood alongside his earlier self-hardening?

views.label: A confirming judgment
views.case: By this stage Pharaoh has hardened his own heart repeatedly through nine plagues; God's hardening here confirms him in the rebellion he has already and consistently chosen.

views.label: A sovereign act for God's glory
views.case: The text states plainly that God hardens Pharaoh so that He will gain honor through Pharaoh's defeat, showing God's sovereign freedom to use even human rebellion to display His own glory.

debates.bearing: Both readings affirm real human responsibility and real divine sovereignty together; Pharaoh is never portrayed as an innocent puppet, and God's purposes are never thwarted by human defiance.

study.originalLanguages: Moses promises the people will see the LORD's yeshuah, salvation, a word that becomes, through Greek translation, the very name of Jesus. What Israel learns at the sea is to yare, fear, God rightly rather than fear Egypt, and to aman, believe, trust Him as the ground of their security, not their own strength.

typology.type: Israel's crossing through the sea, judgment on one side, deliverance on the other
typology.fulfillment: Paul calls it a baptism into Moses, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2, a pattern reapplied to Christian baptism, passing through judgment into new life united with Christ.

typology.type: Moses stretching out his hand to save the helpless
typology.fulfillment: A picture of the mediator's role, fulfilled fully in Christ, who alone accomplishes the salvation His people could never achieve, Hebrews 3:1-6.

study.christ: The New Testament invites us to read this crossing through a Christ-centered lens: bondage in Egypt echoes bondage to sin, the sea is a barrier only a mediator can overcome, and the crossing itself becomes, in Paul's words, a baptism into Moses, prefiguring baptism into Christ, 1 Corinthians 10:1-2. Just as Israel stood still and watched the LORD fight, salvation in Christ is received by faith in what He has done, not achieved by human striving.
study.god: God fights for His people when they can do nothing but stand still, and He turns the very pursuit meant to destroy Israel into the means of His own glory and Egypt's undoing. His patience with Israel's fear at the shore does not cancel His demand that they trust and move forward; grace and calling to faith go together.

crossReferences.note: Paul calls Israel's passage through the sea a baptism into Moses, a type later fulfilled in Christian baptism.
The prophet recalls this very crossing as the pattern for future redemption and the ransomed returning to Zion.
Retells the crossing to teach later generations both God's power to save and Israel's short-lived faith.
Names this crossing an act of Israel's faith, by which they passed through the Red Sea as on dry land.

study.oneStory: Trapped with no way out, Israel is told to stand still and watch God alone fight and save. This is the Bible's clearest Old Testament picture of salvation by grace: a helpless people, an impossible barrier, and a mediator through whom God delivers, a pattern that reaches its fulfillment when Christ alone secures the deliverance His people could never win for themselves.

sermon.title: The Great Escape
sermon.why: Preached on Exodus 14:11-31, Keller draws out how the Red Sea crossing pictures salvation as bondage with layers, a crossing accomplished by grace, and a mediator through whom deliverance comes, reading the whole episode through a Christ-centered lens.

quiz.q: Why did God lead Israel by the longer wilderness road instead of the shorter route through Philistine territory?

options.text: So that facing war too soon would not make the people change their minds and return to Egypt.
options.note: Verse 17 gives this exact reason for God's chosen route.

options.text: Because the shorter road no longer existed.
options.note: The text gives a pastoral, protective reason, not a geographical impossibility.

options.text: Because Moses preferred the wilderness route personally.
options.note: The decision is explicitly God's, made for the people's own good, not Moses' personal preference.

options.text: There is no stated reason for the route chosen.
options.note: Verse 17 explicitly explains God's reasoning.

quiz.q: What did Moses tell the terrified people to do as Pharaoh's army approached?

options.text: Stand firm, be still, and watch the LORD fight for them and save them.
options.note: Verses 13 to 14 give this exact instruction, faith expressed as stillness before God's action.

options.text: Arm themselves and prepare to fight the Egyptian army.
options.note: Moses tells them the opposite: the LORD will fight for them, they need only be still.

options.text: Scatter into the wilderness in different directions.
options.note: Moses calls for unified trust and stillness, not scattering in panic.

options.text: Surrender immediately to Pharaoh's forces.
options.note: Moses promises deliverance, not surrender.

quiz.q: How did the LORD divide the sea, according to verse 21?

options.text: By driving it back all that night with a strong east wind, so the waters were divided and the ground became dry.
options.note: The text specifies this means, a strong east wind, alongside Moses stretching out his hand.

options.text: By an earthquake that split the seabed instantly.
options.note: The text specifies a strong wind driving back the water overnight, not an earthquake.

options.text: The sea simply evaporated over several days.
options.note: The text describes a single overnight event, not a gradual evaporation.

options.text: The text does not describe how the sea was divided.
options.note: Verse 21 gives a specific description of the means, wind driving back the waters.

quiz.q: What happened to the pursuing Egyptian army?

options.text: The sea returned over them and not one of them survived.
options.note: Verse 28 states this outcome plainly.

options.text: They turned back safely before entering the seabed.
options.note: The Egyptians did follow into the sea, verse 23, and were destroyed when the waters returned.

options.text: They were captured and enslaved by Israel.
options.note: The text describes destruction by the returning sea, not capture.

options.text: Only Pharaoh himself drowned; the rest of the army survived.
options.note: The text says not one of the pursuing army survived.

quiz.q: What was the result in Israel after seeing this display of God's power?

options.text: They feared the LORD and believed in Him and in His servant Moses.
options.note: Verse 31 states this directly, fear and faith flowing from what they had witnessed.

options.text: They immediately began to worship the Egyptian gods out of terror.
options.note: The opposite occurs; they fear and believe in the LORD, not Egypt's gods.

options.text: They demanded to return to Egypt at once.
options.note: That grumbling recurs later in the wilderness, but this verse describes a moment of fear and faith, not a request to return.

options.text: No lasting response is recorded.
options.note: Verse 31 explicitly records their fear and belief.

questions: Israel panicked the moment the danger looked overwhelming, even after everything they had already seen God do. What overwhelming situation right now is tempting you to forget what God has already proven about Himself?
Moses told the people that all they had to do was stand still and watch the LORD fight for them. Where do you need to stop striving in your own strength and simply trust God to act on your behalf?

God deliberately chose the longer, harder road to protect Israel from a fight they were not ready for. Can you see a hard or slow path in your own life that might actually be God's protection rather than His neglect?

The Egyptians said, 'the LORD is fighting for them against Egypt,' even as they drowned. What does it mean for you that God's power to save His people is inseparable from His power to judge those who oppose them?

Fear and faith are named together as Israel's response at the shore, a right fear of God producing real trust. How might a deeper reverence for who God is actually strengthen, rather than weaken, your faith this week?

translationNotes.note: Arrayed for battle can also be rendered in orderly ranks or by fifties, describing an organized departure rather than a chaotic flight, though the Hebrew term's precise meaning is debated.
The place names Pi-hahiroth, Migdol, and Baal-zephon are precise enough that ancient readers could locate the crossing, though their exact modern identification remains uncertain.

A strong east wind is a genuine meteorological description, yet the text is clear the timing, intensity, and result were entirely under God's sovereign control, an act of both nature and miracle together.
